title: AI Agents to Make Sense of Data at OpenAI
gist: OpenAI engineer Bonnie Xu details Kepler, an internal AI data-analyst agent querying 600+ petabytes across 70k datasets, explaining how context engineering (Codex-crawled table metadata, RAG, scoped memory) and AST-based LLM eval grading make it accurate ~most of the time and adopted across the whole company.

insights.claim: At OpenAI, 80% of the company directly uses the data platform's 15 tools to process over 600 petabytes of data per day across 70,000 total datasets, a scale at which table discovery and semantic nuance (encrypted vs unencrypted IDs, fraud-adjusted columns, pre-filtered tables) become the core analytics bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: Quantifies internal AI/data scale at a frontier lab and pinpoints metadata/discovery as the binding constraint — a market signal for semantic-layer and data-catalog tooling.
insights.quote: 80% of the company using our team's 15 tools to process over 600 petabytes of data a day across 70k total datasets

insights.claim: Missing a single data nuance at OpenAI's scale can produce answers wrong by an order of magnitude, which Xu calls catastrophic for important business decisions.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: Reliability/accuracy is the gating risk for AI analytics adoption; validation tooling is where trust is won or lost.
insights.quote: Missing one nuance can lead to an answer that is wrong by an order of magnitude
insights.locator: Some tables don't. Some tables are pre-filtered

insights.claim: Kepler cannot fit 70k table schemas into a model context window, so OpenAI preprocesses table metadata (schemas, query history, lineage) into embeddings via the OpenAI API for runtime retrieval, treating context construction as the primary lever rather than the model itself.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: Reinforces the recurring lesson that retrieval/context engineering, not raw model power, drives enterprise AI accuracy — relevant to vector-DB and RAG infrastructure demand.
insights.quote: fitting all 70k tables with their schemas and query history is just too much data to put into a model's context window
insights.locator: As a start, we need table metadata context

insights.claim: OpenAI runs daily parallel Codex tasks that crawl the codebase to auto-generate rich table context — purpose, downstream usage, grain, primary keys, freshness, lineage — so the agent understands, for example, that a table holds first-party ChatGPT traffic enriched by safety signals, eliminating tedious manual metadata maintenance across 70k tables.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: Using a coding agent to keep semantic metadata fresh is a reusable pattern; signals automation displacing manual data-governance labor.
insights.quote: These Codex tasks are launched in parallel daily. They crawl the codebase to understand things like a table's purpose, downstream usage patterns, exact grain and primary keys, the freshness
insights.locator: We achieve this by essentially running an offline job

insights.claim: Kepler uses MCP to give the agent a toolbox it can iteratively re-invoke — internal knowledge search, schema checks, query runs — so the agent reasons by itself, gets feedback from its own tool calls, and self-corrects (e.g., redoing a join with the wrong key) while carrying context across steps.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: Validates MCP as production-grade agent tooling at a frontier lab; supports the thesis that standardized tool protocols are durable infrastructure.
insights.locator: explain why using MCP has been so helpful for us

insights.claim: OpenAI uses scoped semantic memory (user, channel/team, and global) so corrections persist — e.g., that 'daily active users' means users with at least one message excluding external in PST — letting the agent reproduce the right query each time; bad memories are pruned offline and the model can disregard incorrect retrieved memories.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: Memory-as-continuous-learning is the mechanism turning an LLM tool into a self-improving system; a design pattern for enterprise agent products.
insights.quote: memory is really the mechanism that helps the agent continuously learn and improve
insights.locator: Memory is useful for things like corrections and learnings

insights.claim: Kepler's evals compare AI-generated SQL and result sets against manually curated expected answers using an OpenAI LLM grader, normalizing both to AST representation because exact SQL text equality is a poor pass/fail signal and allowing graded wiggle room on result differences that don't change meaning.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: AST-based LLM-graded evals are an emerging regression-prevention pattern for code/SQL-generating agents; informs how to evaluate agentic products.
insights.quote: exact SQL text equality isn't really a good representation, whether a SQL eval passed
insights.locator: Here are some key takeaways from our evals process

insights.claim: Quoting Greg Brockman, Xu says 'evals are surprisingly often all you need,' framing a robust regression-free evaluation pipeline as the key to maintaining trust in an AI analytics agent.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: Eval infrastructure is treated as the make-or-break investment for production AI; a heuristic for assessing AI-product maturity.
insights.quote: evals are surprisingly often all you need
insights.locator: In the words of the wise Greg Brockman

insights.claim: Giving the model too many overlapping or similar tool calls confused Kepler, so OpenAI ratcheted down available tool calls for easier tool discovery, and found overly prescriptive instructions hurt results — they made prompting more general and leaned on GPT-5's reasoning to pick the path.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive design lessons — fewer tools and less-prescriptive prompts — guide how to build reliable agents on capable reasoning models.
insights.quote: if you give the model too much information, especially when it's overlapping, it can get really confused
insights.locator: It turns out if you give the model too much information

insights.claim: OpenAI deliberately built Kepler's first interface on Slack rather than web because OpenAI is a Slack-heavy company where analytics questions already happen, and meeting users where they are drove adoption from data scientists out to GTM, finance, econ, and product teams company-wide.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: Distribution-channel choice (existing workflow surface) is a key adoption driver for internal AI tools — a go-to-market lesson.
insights.quote: the key to success was actually getting people to ping Kepler with their analytics questions
insights.locator: The other thing that really helped us was meeting users where they are

insights.claim: Kepler does not grant extra authorization — it passes through existing authentication, redacts sensitive Slack outputs via an internal PII anonymization service, and ingests pre-sanitized queries — and OpenAI plans to fine-tune a Kepler-dedicated model and add self-validation steps where Kepler cross-checks its number against a dashboard.
insights.speaker: Bonnie Xu
insights.so_what: Security-by-passthrough plus planned fine-tuning shows the maturation path for enterprise data agents; relevant to data-governance and AI-security demand.
insights.locator: How Are We Doing This Safely?
